content.problemStatement:
You are given an array AAA with NNN integers. You have to tell Chef whether this array is closed under multiplication.

We say that an array is closed under multiplication if, for all sequences 1≤S1<S2<⋯<Sk≤N1 \leq S_1 \lt S_2 \lt \dots \lt S_k \leq N1≤S1​<S2​<⋯<Sk​≤N,  there exists 1≤x≤N1 \leq x \leq N1≤x≤N such that AS1⋅AS2⋅⋯⋅ASk=AxA_{S_1} \cdot A_{S_2} \cdot \dots \cdot A_{S_k} = A_xAS1​​⋅AS2​​⋅⋯⋅ASk​​=Ax​.

Output 1 if it is closed, and 0 otherwise.


Input Format

The first line of the input contains TTT - the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer - NNN
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers - the ithi^{th}ith of which is AiA_iAi​


Output Format
For each test case, output the answer on a new line. Output 111 if AAA is closed under multiplication, and 000 otherwise.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 10^6
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains an integer N, the size of the array. Second line contains N space‑separated integers A1 … AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing 1 if the array is closed under multiplication, otherwise 0.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: [1,0,3][1, 0, 3][1,0,3] is closed under multiplication.
A1⋅A2=1⋅0=0=A2A_1 \cdot A_2 = 1 \cdot 0 = 0 = A_2A1​⋅A2​=1⋅0=0=A2​
A1⋅A2⋅A3=1⋅0⋅3=0=A2A_1 \cdot A_2 \cdot A_3 = 1 \cdot 0 \cdot 3 = 0 = A_2A1​⋅A2​⋅A3​=1⋅0⋅3=0=A2​
A1⋅A3=1⋅3=3=A3A_1 \cdot A_3 = 1 \cdot 3 = 3 = A_3A1​⋅A3​=1⋅3=3=A3​
A2⋅A3=0⋅3=0=A2A_2 \cdot A_3 = 0 \cdot 3 = 0 = A_2A2​⋅A3​=0⋅3=0=A2​
Therefore, the answer for the first test case is 111.
Test case 222: [1,−1,−1][1, -1, -1][1,−1,−1] is closed under multiplication.
A1⋅A2=1⋅−1=−1=A2A_1 \cdot A_2 = 1 \cdot -1 = -1 = A_2A1​⋅A2​=1⋅−1=−1=A2​
A1⋅A2⋅A3=1⋅−1⋅−1=1=A1A_1 \cdot A_2 \cdot A_3 = 1 \cdot -1 \cdot -1 = 1 = A_1A1​⋅A2​⋅A3​=1⋅−1⋅−1=1=A1​
A1⋅A3=1⋅−1=−1=A2A_1 \cdot A_3 = 1 \cdot -1 = -1 = A_2A1​⋅A3​=1⋅−1=−1=A2​
A2⋅A3=−1⋅−1=1=A1A_2 \cdot A_3 = -1 \cdot -1 = 1 = A_1A2​⋅A3​=−1⋅−1=1=A1​
Therefore, the answer for the second test case is 111.
Test case 333: A2⋅A3=3⋅3=9A_2 \cdot A_3 = 3 \cdot 3 = 9A2​⋅A3​=3⋅3=9 is not present in the array. Therefore, [1,3,3][1, 3, 3][1,3,3] is not closed under multiplication, and the answer is 000.

solution.approach: Let cnt0, cnt1, cntMinus1 be the numbers of 0, 1 and -1 in the array. Let big be the count of elements whose absolute value is greater than 1. The array is closed iff all of the following hold: 1) big ≤ 1 (more than one such element would need their product, which is a new element). 2) If big == 1 then cntMinus1 == 0 (otherwise -1 * big would be a new element). 3) If cntMinus1 > 1 then cnt1 ≥ 1 (product of two -1 is 1, which must exist). All other combinations are safe because 0·x = 0 and 1·x = x are already present. The algorithm counts these categories in O(N) per test case and checks the three conditions.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, total O(∑N) ≤ 10^6.
